name: The Runge–Kutta group
statement_text:
Let α and β be multiplicative mappings from the forests to
the real numbers. Then αβ is multiplicative.
proof_text:
Proof. It will be sufficient to consider the value of (αβ)(S), where S = S1 ∪S2 .
Each R  S can be written as R = R1 ∪ R2 , where R1  S1 and R2  S2 . We
now have
                      
          (αβ)(S) =       α(S \ R)β(R)
                       R S
                                                  
                   =           α(S1 \ R1 )β(R1 )           α(S2 \ R2 )β(R2 )
                       R1 S1                       R2 S2

                   = (αβ)(S1 )(αβ)(S2 ).

variables.name: α
variables.definition: multiplicative mapping from forests to real numbers

variables.name: β
variables.definition: multiplicative mapping from forests to real numbers

variables.name: αβ
variables.definition: product mapping defined by (αβ)(S) = Σ_{R ≤ S} α(S \ R)β(R)

variables.name: S
variables.definition: a forest (represented by a single letter)

variables.name: R
variables.definition: a forest such that R ≤ S

variables.name: S \ R
variables.definition: the forest induced by the difference of the vertex sets of S and R

preamble: Forests (rooted trees) are denoted by single letters; a relation R ≤ S indicates R is a sub-forest of S. Multiplicative mappings from forests to reals are extended multiplicatively from trees to forests. The product of two such mappings α and β is defined via a sum over sub-forests.
